message.content:
You are an adversarial security reviewer. Your job is to REFUTE the following candidate vulnerability. Default to refuted=true unless the evidence is solid. Read the actual code at the cited path before deciding.

TARGET: weblate  (repo root: C:/bugBounty/targets/weblate)
THREAT MODEL: VCS layer that runs git/hg/ssh/gpg subprocesses with arguments derived from project configuration (repo URL, branch, push URL, credentials) set by project admins (a lower-privilege role than superuser) and sometimes from remote repo content. Watch for argument injection (values beginning with "-" or "--", git "ext::"/"fd::" transports, "file://"), SSRF via remote URL fetch, and credential exposure in process args/logs.

CANDIDATE FINDING:
  title: Authenticated push URL containing credentials written to logs at INFO level
  class: Credential exposure (CWE-532)
  severity claimed: Low
  file: weblate/vcs/git.py:1422
  source: GitMergeRequestBase.configure_fork_remote() builds push_url via authenticate_url(http_url, credentials), embedding the configured username and API token as 'username:token@host'. Token originates from the *_CREDENTIALS settings (e.g. GITHUB_CREDENTIALS[host]['token']).
  sink: self.log(f"Configuring fork remote '{remote_name}': {push_url}", level=logging.INFO) -> weblate.vcs logger -> log aggregation / Sentry breadcrumbs
  data_flow: get_credentials() reads token from settings -> push() -> fork()/configure_fork_remote(ssh_url, http_url, credentials) -> push_url = authenticate_url(http_url, credentials) (URL now contains plaintext token, git.py:1493-1502) -> self.log(... push_url, level=INFO) at git.py:1422. Emitted whenever a merge-request backend configures its fork remote during a push.
  excerpt: (none)

LENS = CONCRETE EXPLOIT. Construct the precise input/request/payload that triggers the issue and state the resulting impact. If you cannot produce a plausible working exploit from the actual code (or it requires unrealistic preconditions), set refuted=true.

Set is_real=true ONLY if, after reading the code, the finding survives your lens. Provide reason and, if real, an exploit_sketch and adjusted_severity. Return ONLY the structured object.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- contribute:contribute: This skill should be used when the user asks to "contribute to open source", "find an issue to work on", "analyze a repo", "submit a pull request", "review PR feedback", "triage an issue", "sync my fork", "create a release", "clean up contribution branches", "debug CI failures", or mentions open-source contribution workflows. Provides a full-lifecycle open-source contribution system with 12 phases.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: There IS a `sanitize_error_message` but the `log()` method at base.py:351 does NOT sanitize — it logs the message verbatim. So the push_url with embedded token reaches the logger unredacted. Let me confirm sanitize is only applied to errors, not to `log()`.

content.text:
The base `sanitize_error_message` is a no-op anyway. The `log()` method clearly emits the full message including the token URL at INFO level. This is reachable whenever a merge-request backend (GitHub/GitLab/Pagure/Gitea/Bitbucket) configures its fork remote during push.

Assessment: The finding is accurate. The token is plaintext (only URL-quoted, not redacted) in an INFO log line. Source is credentials from `*_CREDENTIALS` settings. This is a genuine CWE-532 credential exposure. The "concrete exploit" lens: this isn't attacker-injected — it's a configuration/logging hygiene issue. Severity is Low (requires log access; tokens are already in settings). It's real but low impact.

input.reason: Confirmed by reading the code. configure_fork_remote() at git.py:1413-1424 builds push_url via authenticate_url() (git.py:1493-1502), which embeds username:token@host using only urllib.parse.quote (URL-encoding, not redaction). The resulting URL still contains the plaintext API token. It is passed to self.log(f"Configuring fork remote '{remote_name}': {push_url}", level=logging.INFO). The base log() method (base.py:351-352) does NOT sanitize: it calls LOGGER.log(level, "%s: %s", cls._cmd, message) verbatim. sanitize_error_message (base.py:547-548) is a no-op and is only applied to error paths (base.py:502,527), never to log(). So the token reaches the weblate.vcs logger at INFO whenever a merge-request backend (GitHub/GitLab/Gitea/Pagure/Bitbucket) configures its fork remote during push, and the http push_scheme branch is taken. The token comes from *_CREDENTIALS settings (e.g. GITHUB_CREDENTIALS[host]['token']). This is a legitimate CWE-532 credential-exposure / logging-hygiene defect. It is not attacker-controlled and not an injection; impact requires read access to logs / Sentry breadcrumbs where the secret is then exposed, so Low severity is accurate.
input.exploit_sketch: Configure a component to push via a merge-request backend (e.g. GitHub) with GITHUB_CREDENTIALS[host] = {username, token}, where the push_scheme resolves to http(s) rather than ssh. Trigger a push (Weblate auto-push or admin "Push" action). push() -> fork() -> configure_fork_remote(ssh_url, http_url, credentials) executes the else-branch (push_url = authenticate_url(http_url, credentials)), producing https://username:TOKEN@host/.... self.log(..., level=logging.INFO) emits this line to the weblate.vcs logger. Anyone with read access to application logs or Sentry breadcrumbs (ops staff, log-aggregation users, or an attacker who has compromised log storage) recovers the plaintext API token and can act as that user against the upstream Git host. No malicious input is required; the secret leak is the impact.

content.text: Finding confirmed as real (low severity). The token-bearing push URL is logged at INFO without redaction at `weblate/vcs/git.py:1422`, with the no-op sanitizer at `base.py:547` and the verbatim `log()` at `base.py:351`.
